title_en: Nine Needles and the Twelve Source Points
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo, saying: 'I care for the myriad people as my children, nurturing the common folk and collecting their taxes and levies. Yet I grieve that they lack sufficiency and so fall prey to illness. I wish to spare them from being subjected to poisonous medicines and from the use of stone needles, and instead to use the fine needle to open through their channels, to regulate their qi and blood, and to govern the confluences where these flow contrary to or in accord with their proper course, entering and emerging. I wish this to be transmitted to later generations, and it must therefore be set forth with a clear method, so that it may come to an end without being extinguished, endure long without being severed, be easy to use yet not easily forgotten. Let it be given order and system, let its sections be distinguished, its exterior and interior differentiated, and let it be given a beginning and an end. Let each part have its proper form, and let a Canon of Needling first be established. I wish to hear its particulars.'

Qibo replied: 'Allow your servant to set these forth in proper sequence, so that they may have order and system, beginning with one and ending with nine. Let me speak of their principles. The essentials of the fine needle are easy to state but difficult to master. The unskilled practitioner holds fast to the outward form; the superior practitioner holds fast to the spirit. How subtle is the spirit — the invading evil lodges at the gate! Without first perceiving the illness, how can one know its origin? The subtlety of needling lies in its timing — whether swift or slow. The unskilled practitioner holds fast to the joints and bony landmarks; the superior practitioner holds fast to the pivotal moment. The movement of this pivot never departs from the aperture. Within the aperture the pivot is pure, still, and subtle — one cannot meet it as it comes, nor pursue it as it goes. One who understands the way of the pivot will not be off by so much as a hair's breadth; one who does not understand the way of the pivot, though he presses upon it, will not release it. To know its coming and going, one must engage it at the precise moment. How obscure this is to the unskilled — how marvelous! — and it is the skilled practitioner alone who possesses it. What departs is called contrary; what arrives is called compliant. Once one clearly knows the contrary and the compliant, one may proceed directly without hesitation. If one meets the qi head-on and seizes it, how can this fail to create deficiency? If one follows behind and reinforces it, how can this fail to create excess? Whether meeting it or following it, one harmonizes it through the intention of the mind — and the way of the needle is then complete.'

In general, in the use of the needle: where there is deficiency, one fills it; where there is fullness, one drains it; where there is stagnant old blood, one removes it; where evil qi prevails, one depletes it. The great essential is this: to insert slowly and withdraw swiftly produces repletion; to insert swiftly and withdraw slowly produces depletion. In speaking of repletion and depletion, the sensation is as though something were present, as though nothing were there. One examines what comes after and what comes before, as though something remained, as though it had vanished. In bringing about depletion and repletion, it is as though one had grasped something, as though one had lost it.

Of the essentials of depletion and repletion, the nine needles are the most marvelous, and the timing of supplementation and drainage is accomplished through the needle. In drainage, one must grasp the needle firmly and insert it, then release and withdraw it; as the yang is dispersed the needle is felt to loosen, and the evil qi is thereby drained. If instead one presses down and draws the needle out, this is called 'warming within' — the blood cannot disperse, and the qi cannot be released. In supplementation, one follows the qi again and again, applying the mind as though heedlessly, now moving, now pressing, alighting as lightly as a mosquito or gadfly, lingering as though staying and returning, withdrawing the needle as abruptly as a snapped bowstring, causing the left hand to press where the right hand withdraws, so that the qi is thereby retained. Once the outer gate is closed, the qi within becomes replete, and there must be no lingering blood — if there is, one must quickly remove it.

The way of holding the needle: firmness is its treasure. Point the finger straight and puncture directly, without slanting the needle to the left or right. Let the spirit be fixed on the finest hair's tip, and fasten the mind upon the patient. Only after carefully examining the blood vessels may one needle without danger. At the moment of needling, one's attention must rest upon the 'suspended yang' and its two guardian points. Keep the spirit fastened there without wavering, so as to know whether the disease is present or gone. Where the blood vessels lie crosswise over the transport points, on inspection they alone appear clear and distinct, and on palpation they alone feel firm.

The names of the nine needles, each differing in form: the first is called the chan needle, one cun six fen in length (a cun being a proportional unit of body measurement, and a fen one-tenth of a cun); the second is called the yuan needle, one cun six fen in length; the third is called the di needle, three cun and a half in length; the fourth is called the feng needle, one cun six fen in length; the fifth is called the pi needle, four cun in length and two fen and a half in width; the sixth is called the yuanli needle, one cun six fen in length; the seventh is called the hao needle, three cun six fen in length; the eighth is called the chang needle, seven cun in length; the ninth is called the da needle, four cun in length. The chan needle has a large head and a sharp point, and is used to drain the yang qi. The yuan needle is shaped like an egg, and is rubbed between the intervals of the flesh without injuring the muscles, so as to drain the qi of the interstices. The di needle has a tip as sharp as a millet grain, and its chief use is to press upon the vessel without letting it sink in, so as to bring the qi to arrive. The feng needle has a three-sided blade, and is used to release stubborn and chronic disease. The pi needle has a tip like the edge of a sword, and is used to remove large accumulations of pus. The yuanli needle is as thick as a horsehair, both round and sharp, slightly thicker toward the middle of its shaft, and is used to draw out sudden and violent qi. The hao needle has a tip as fine as the proboscis of a mosquito or gadfly; it is inserted quietly and slowly, and retained gently for a long while so as to nourish the qi, and is used to treat painful impediment. The chang needle is sharp-edged and thin in body, and can be used to treat impediment lodged deep within. The da needle has a tip like a stick, its point slightly rounded, and is used to drain water accumulated at the joints. Thus the nine needles are complete.

Now, as for the qi within the vessels: evil qi lodges above, turbid qi lodges in the middle, and clear qi lodges below. Thus if one needles the sunken vessel, the evil qi is expelled; if one needles the middle of the vessel, the turbid qi is expelled; but if one needles too deeply, the evil qi is instead driven further down, and the disease worsens. Hence it is said: the skin, flesh, sinews, and vessels each have their proper location, and each disease has its proper treatment. Since the nine needles each differ in form, each should be employed according to what is proper to it — one should neither create excess nor deficiency. To further diminish what is already insufficient, or to further augment what is already in excess, is called aggravating the disease. When disease is thereby aggravated, drawing qi from the vessels of all five viscera causes death, and drawing qi from the vessels of three of the viscera causes debility. To rob a patient of yin causes death; to rob a patient of yang causes derangement — such are the harms of reckless needling, now fully stated.

When one needles and the qi does not arrive, do not count the number of insertions. When one needles and the qi arrives, then withdraw the needle, and do not needle again. Each needle has that to which it is suited, each differing in form, each employed according to its proper place — this is the essential of needling. When the qi arrives there is efficacy, and the sign of this efficacy is as when the wind blows the clouds away, leaving the blue sky clearly visible — and the way of needling is thus complete.

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear of the places from which the qi of the five viscera and six bowels emerges.' Qibo said: 'The five viscera each have five transport points, five times five making twenty-five transport points in all; the six bowels each have six transport points, six times six making thirty-six transport points in all. There are twelve channel vessels and fifteen network vessels, making in all twenty-seven qi that travel up and down the body. The point where the qi emerges is called the well point; where it flows is called the spring point; where it pours in is called the stream point; where it travels is called the river point; and where it enters is called the sea point. The travels of these twenty-seven qi all take place among these five transport points.'

The junctures of the joints number three hundred and sixty-five confluences in all. One who knows the essential of this may sum it up in a single phrase; one who does not know the essential will be scattered and adrift without end. What is meant by 'joints' here are the places where the spirit qi travels, emerges, and enters — they are not merely the skin, flesh, sinews, and bones.

Observe the patient's complexion, examine the eyes, and thereby know whether the spirit is scattered or restored. Steady the patient's posture, attend to his movement and stillness, and thereby know whether the qi is evil or upright. The right hand governs the advancing of the needle, the left hand holds and guides it; once the qi arrives, withdraw the needle.

Whenever one is about to use the needle, one must first diagnose the pulse, observe whether the qi's condition is severe or mild, and only then may one proceed to treat. If the qi of the five viscera has already been cut off within, and the practitioner instead reinforces the exterior, this is called 'redoubled exhaustion.' Redoubled exhaustion must result in death, and such a death is quiet and still. Yet the practitioner who does this instead reverses the qi still further, by needling the armpit and chest. If the qi of the five viscera has already been cut off in the exterior, and the practitioner instead reinforces the interior, this is called 'contrary reversal.' Contrary reversal must also result in death, and such a death is agitated and restless. Yet the practitioner who does this instead needles the four extremities.

In needling, if harm reaches the interior and the needle is not withdrawn, the essence is thereby leaked away; if harm reaches the interior and the needle is promptly withdrawn, the qi is thereby brought to arrive. If the essence is leaked away, the disease grows more severe and the patient becomes debilitated; if the qi is brought to arrive in this manner, welling-abscesses and sores may result.

The five viscera are matched with the six bowels, and the six bowels have twelve source points. These twelve source points emerge at the four joints of the limbs, and the four joints govern the treatment of the five viscera. When the five viscera fall ill, one should select points among the twelve source points, for these twelve source points are the means by which the five viscera receive the qi and flavor of the three hundred and sixty-five joints of the body. When the five viscera fall ill, this will manifest at the twelve source points, and each of the twelve source points has its own place of emergence. By clearly knowing these source points and observing their responses, one may know the harm done to the five viscera. Within yang, the lesser yin is the lung, whose source point emerges at Taiyuan — there being two Taiyuan points. Within yang, the greater yang is the heart, whose source point emerges at Daling — there being two Daling points. Within yin, the lesser yang is the liver, whose source point emerges at Taichong — there being two Taichong points. Within yin, the utmost yin is the spleen, whose source point emerges at Taibai — there being two Taibai points. Within yin, the greater yin is the kidney, whose source point emerges at Taixi — there being two Taixi points. The source point of the fat membranes emerges at Jiuwei, there being one Jiuwei point. The source point of the vital membranes emerges at Boyang, there being one Boyang point. All these twelve source points govern the treatment of illnesses of the five viscera and six bowels.
For distention, select points among the three yang; for diarrhea with undigested food, select points among the three yin.

Now, when the five viscera fall ill, it is like being pierced by a thorn, like being stained by filth, like being tied in a knot, like being blocked and obstructed. Though the thorn has been embedded long, it can still be drawn out; though the stain has lingered long, it can still be washed clean; though the knot has been tied long, it can still be untied; though the obstruction has persisted long, it can still be cleared. Some say that long-standing illness cannot be treated — this is not a correct teaching. Now, one who is skilled in the use of the needle, in removing illness, does so as one draws out a thorn, as one washes away a stain, as one unties a knot, as one clears an obstruction. Though the illness be long-standing, it can still be brought to an end. To say that it cannot be treated is simply to have not yet mastered the technique.

In needling for various heat conditions, one should withdraw the needle as swiftly as if testing scalding water with the hand; in needling for cold conditions, one should linger as if a person were reluctant to walk. When there is a yang-type illness within the yin, select the point Xiaxiling — that is, Zusanli; proceed directly and without hesitation, and stop once the qi descends; if it does not descend, begin again. When the illness is located high in the body but affects the interior, select the point Yinlingquan; when the illness is located high in the body but affects the exterior, select the point Yanglingquan.

Huangdi asked Qibo, saying: 'In the way of needling generally, one must thoroughly understand the beginning and end of the twelve channels and their network vessels, the places where the network vessels branch off, the places where the five transport points reside, the bowels with which each channel unites, the seasonal points of emergence and entry, the places where the qi of the five viscera flows, the measure of their breadth and number, the manner of their shallowness and depth, and the heights and depths to which they extend. I wish to hear this explained.'

Qibo said: 'Allow me to set them forth in order. The lung channel emerges at Shaoshang — Shaoshang lies at the inner side of the tip of the thumb — and is the wood well point. It flows into Yuji — Yuji lies at the 'fish' of the hand, the fleshy eminence at the base of the thumb — and is the spring point. It pours into Taiyuan — Taiyuan lies in the depression one cun behind the fish — and is the stream point. It travels through Jingqu — Jingqu lies at the radial pulse of the wrist, where the pulse beats without ceasing — and is the river point. It enters at Chize — Chize lies at the pulsating vessel in the crook of the elbow — and is the sea point. This is the Hand Taiyin channel.'

The heart channel emerges at Zhongchong — Zhongchong lies at the tip of the middle finger — and is the wood well point. It flows into Laogong — Laogong lies in the palm, at the inner side of the base joint of the middle finger — and is the spring point. It pours into Daling — Daling lies just below, between the two bones behind the palm — and is the stream point. It travels through Jianshi — the path of Jianshi lies between the two sinews, three cun above the wrist; when there is a disorder, the pulse arrives there, and when there is none, it does not — and this is the river point. It enters at Quze — Quze lies in the depression on the inner border below the elbow, found by bending the arm — and is the sea point. This is the Hand Shaoyin channel.

The liver channel emerges at Dadun — Dadun lies at the tip of the big toe, at the cluster of three hairs — and is the wood well point. It flows into Xingjian — Xingjian lies between the big toe and the second toe — and is the spring point. It pours into Taichong — Taichong lies in the depression two cun above Xingjian — and is the stream point. It travels through Zhongfeng — Zhongfeng lies in the depression one and a half cun in front of the inner ankle bone; if manipulated against the current the qi becomes congested, if manipulated in accord with the current it flows freely, and the point is found by rotating the foot — and this is the river point. It enters at Ququan — Ququan lies below the rounded bone and above the great sinew, and is found by bending the knee — and is the sea point. This is the Foot Jueyin channel.

The spleen channel emerges at Yinbai — Yinbai lies at the inner side of the tip of the big toe — and is the wood well point. It flows into Dadu — Dadu lies in the depression below and behind the base joint — and is the spring point. It pours into Taibai — Taibai lies below the wrist-bone of the foot — and is the stream point. It travels through Shangqiu — Shangqiu lies in the depression below the inner ankle bone — and is the river point. It enters at Yinlingquan — Yinlingquan lies in the depression below the rounded bone, and is found by extending the leg — and is the sea point. This is the Foot Taiyin channel.

The kidney channel emerges at Yongquan — Yongquan lies at the center of the sole of the foot — and is the wood well point. It flows into Rangu — Rangu lies below the 'blazing bone,' the navicular tuberosity — and is the spring point. It pours into Taixi — Taixi lies in the depression behind the inner ankle bone and above the heel bone — and is the stream point. It travels through Fuliu — Fuliu lies two cun above the inner ankle bone, where the pulse beats without ceasing — and is the river point. It enters at Yingu — Yingu lies behind the rounded bone, below the great sinew and above the small sinew; pressing there the pulse responds to the hand, and it is found by bending the knee — and is the sea point. This is the Foot Shaoyin channel.

The bladder channel emerges at Zhiyin — Zhiyin lies at the tip of the small toe — and is the metal well point. It flows into Tonggu — Tonggu lies at the outer side, in front of the base joint — and is the spring point. It pours into Shugu — Shugu lies in the depression behind the base joint — and is the stream point. It passes through Jinggu — Jinggu lies below the great bone on the outer side of the foot — and is the source point. It travels through Kunlun — Kunlun lies behind the outer ankle bone and above the heel bone — and is the river point. It enters at Weizhong — Weizhong lies at the center of the popliteal crease — and is the sea point, found with the knee bent. This is the Foot Taiyang channel.

The gallbladder channel emerges at Qiaoyin — Qiaoyin lies at the tip of the fourth toe — and is the metal well point. It flows into Xiaxi — Xiaxi lies between the fourth toe and the small toe — and is the spring point. It pours into Linqi — Linqi lies one and a half cun further up, in a depression — and is the stream point. It passes through Qiuxu — Qiuxu lies in the depression in front of and below the outer ankle bone — and is the source point. It travels through Yangfu — Yangfu lies above the outer ankle bone, in front of the rounded bone, at the tip of the 'severed bone' — and is the river point. It enters at Yanglingquan — Yanglingquan lies in the depression at the outer side of the knee, found by extending the leg — and is the sea point. This is the Foot Shaoyang channel.

The stomach channel emerges at Lidui — Lidui lies at the tip of the second toe, on the side next to the big toe — and is the metal well point. It flows into Neiting — Neiting lies between the second and third toes — and is the spring point. It pours into Xiangu — Xiangu lies two cun further up, on the inner side of the middle toe, in a depression — and is the stream point. It passes through Chongyang — Chongyang lies five cun up on the instep, in a depression, and is found by rotating the foot — and is the source point. It travels through Jiexi — Jiexi lies one and a half cun above Chongyang, in a depression — and is the river point. It enters at Xialing, that is, Zusanli — which lies three cun below the knee, at the outer side of the shinbone — and is the sea point. Three cun further below Sanli lies Juxu Shanglian, the Upper Great Void, and three cun further below that lies Juxu Xialian, the Lower Great Void. The large intestine is associated with the upper of these two points, and the small intestine with the lower — this is the Foot Yangming stomach channel. Both the large intestine and the small intestine belong to the stomach, which is the Foot Yangming.

The triple burner, in its upper association, unites with the Hand Shaoyang channel, emerging at Guanchong — Guanchong lies at the tip of the fourth finger — and is the metal well point. It flows into Yemen — Yemen lies between the fourth and small fingers — and is the spring point. It pours into Zhongzhu — Zhongzhu lies in the depression behind the base joint — and is the stream point. It passes through Yangchi — Yangchi lies in the depression above the wrist — and is the source point. It travels through Zhigou — Zhigou lies three cun above the wrist, in the depression between the two bones — and is the river point. It enters at Tianjing — Tianjing lies in the depression above the great bone on the outer side of the elbow, found by bending the elbow — and is the sea point. The lower transport point of the triple burner lies in front of the big toe of the foot and behind the Shaoyang channel, emerging at the outer side of the center of the popliteal fossa, at the point called Weiyang; this is the network point of the Taiyang, and belongs to the Hand Shaoyang channel. The triple burner is that which the Foot Shaoyang and Taiyin channels bring forth, a branch of the Taiyang; five cun above the ankle it branches off, penetrates through the calf, and emerges at Weiyang, joining the main pathway of the Taiyang, entering to network with the bladder, and binding the lower burner. When it is in excess there is closure and difficulty in urination; when it is deficient there is incontinence of urine. For incontinence, one should supplement; for closure and difficulty in urination, one should drain.

The Hand Taiyang small intestine channel, in its upper association, unites with the Hand Taiyang, emerging at Shaoze — Shaoze lies at the tip of the small finger — and is the metal well point. It flows into Qiangu — Qiangu lies in the depression on the outer side of the hand, in front of the base joint — and is the spring point. It pours into Houxi — Houxi lies on the outer side of the hand, behind the base joint — and is the stream point. It passes through Wangu — Wangu lies on the outer side of the hand, in front of the wrist bone — and is the source point. It travels through Yanggu — Yanggu lies in the depression below the pointed bone — and is the river point. It enters at Xiaohai — Xiaohai lies outside the great bone of the inner elbow, half a cun from its tip, in a depression, found by extending the arm — and is the sea point. This is the Hand Taiyang channel.

The large intestine channel, in its upper association, unites with the Hand Yangming, emerging at Shangyang — Shangyang lies at the tip of the index finger — and is the metal well point. It flows into Erjian, in front of the base joint, and is the spring point. It pours into Sanjian, behind the base joint, and is the stream point. It passes through Hegu — Hegu lies between the forking bones of the thumb — and is the source point. It travels through Yangxi — Yangxi lies in the depression between the two sinews — and is the river point. It enters at Quchi, which lies in the depression by the rounded bone on the outer side of the elbow, found by bending the arm — and is the sea point. This is the Hand Yangming channel.

These are called the transport points of the five viscera and six bowels: five times five making twenty-five transport points, and six times six making thirty-six transport points. The six bowels all emerge from the three yang channels of the foot, and in their upper association unite with the channels of the hand.

At the center of the supraclavicular fossa lies the Conception Vessel, at the point called Tiantu. The first vessel beside the Conception Vessel is the pulsating vessel of the Foot Yangming, at the point called Renying. The second vessel is that of the Hand Yangming, at the point called Futu. The third vessel is that of the Hand Taiyang, at the point called Tianchuang. The fourth vessel is that of the Foot Shaoyang, at the point called Tianrong. The fifth vessel is that of the Hand Shaoyang, at the point called Tianyou. The sixth vessel is that of the Foot Taiyang, at the point called Tianzhu. The seventh vessel, at the center of the nape of the neck, is the Governing Vessel, at the point called Fengfu. The pulsating vessel within the armpit belongs to the Hand Taiyin, at the point called Tianfu. Three cun below the armpit belongs to the Hand Jueyin, at the point called Tianchi.

When needling Shangguan, the patient can open the mouth wide but cannot close it. When needling Xiaguan, the patient can close the mouth but cannot open it wide. When needling Dubi, the patient can bend the knee but cannot extend it. When needling both Guan points together, the patient can extend the limb but cannot bend it.

The Foot Yangming channel has its pulsating vessel beside the throat, and its transport point lies within the chest. The Hand Yangming's point lies next to it, outside this transport point, one cun short of the angle of the jaw. The Hand Taiyang's point lies right at the angle of the jaw. The Foot Shaoyang's point lies below the ear, behind the angle of the jaw. The Hand Shaoyang's point emerges behind the ear, above and resting upon the 'completion bone,' the mastoid process. The Foot Taiyang's point lies beside the great sinews of the nape of the neck, at the hairline.
The pulsating vessel of the yin at the forearm lies at Wuli — this is a point among the five transport points that is forbidden to needle.

The lung unites with the large intestine, and the large intestine is the bowel that transmits and conveys. The heart unites with the small intestine, and the small intestine is the bowel that receives and holds. The liver unites with the gallbladder, and the gallbladder is the bowel of central essence. The spleen unites with the stomach, and the stomach is the bowel of the five grains. The kidney unites with the bladder, and the bladder is the bowel of fluids. The Shaoyang is associated with the kidney, and the kidney above connects with the lung, and so the kidney governs these two viscera together. The triple burner is the bowel of the central drainage-ditch, from which the waterways emerge; it is associated with the bladder, and is the solitary bowel among the six bowels — these are the bowels with which the six bowels are united.

In spring, select points among the network vessels, the spring points, and the intervals between the flesh along the great channels; where the disease is severe, needle deeply, and where it is mild, needle shallowly. In summer, select points among the stream points and the grandchild network vessels, at the surface of the muscles, flesh, and skin. In autumn, select points among the sea points, otherwise following the method of spring. In winter, select points among the well points and the stream points, needling deeply and retaining the needle long. This is the proper sequence of the four seasons, corresponding to where the qi resides, where the disease lodges, and what is suited to the viscera. For cramping of the sinews, needle the patient standing, and the cramp may thereby be resolved at once. For wilting weakness and reversal, needle the patient with the limb stretched out, and relief may thereby be obtained immediately.
